title: The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic Symptomatology: A Retrospective Online Study
description:
Background: Previous research has shown that interpersonal trauma is associated with higher rates of posttraumatic stress symptoms, with coping responses playing a vital role in individual differences in symptom presentation. The present online study examined differential associations between trauma type, coping styles, and trauma-related symptomatology to deepen understanding of variation in trauma-related symptomatology.
Methods: The sample included N = 300 participants who reported a traumatic life event within the past 18 months, assessed with the Life Events Checklist for DSM-5 (LEC-5). Events were classified into interpersonal and non-interpersonal trauma. Participants completed retrospective questions assessing posttraumatic coping strategies, current levels of posttraumatic stress symptoms, depression, anxiety, stress, and life satisfaction.
Results: The present study reports secondary analyses. Correlational analyses within both trauma groups revealed positive relationships between avoidant coping and trauma-related symptoms. Group comparisons revealed higher levels of trauma-related symptoms, lower life satisfaction, and greater use of avoidant coping strategies in the interpersonal trauma group compared to the non-interpersonal trauma group. Furthermore, avoidant coping explained a significant proportion of variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms within both trauma groups, and trauma type itself accounted for significant unique variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms across the entire sample. Finally, exploratory mediation analyses provided evidence consistent with a partial mediation of the association between trauma type and posttraumatic stress symptoms.
Conclusions: These findings suggest that avoidant coping may be an important factor in understanding differences in trauma-related symptoms across trauma types. The results also point to potential intervention and prevention targets that should be evaluated in longitudinal research.

title: Emotional and physical abuse have dissociable impacts on punishment learning and task engagement during adolescence
description: Childhood adversity is a robust risk factor for psychopathology, and growing evidence implicates developmental alterations in reward and punishment learning as potent mechanisms shaping this vulnerability. However, little is known about how specific types of adversity impact different learning processes, particularly during the sensitive adolescent period. To examine effects of different types of adversity on the development of valenced learning processes, we combined a subtype-specific approach to childhood adversity, a behavioral task and reinforcement learning (RL) model that capture distinct component processes of learning, and a longitudinal design in youth aged 9-17 (n = 166). Task performance metrics included traditional summary scores and RL-derived estimates for reward learning rate, punishment learning rate, win/loss frequency sensitivity, and go bias (general tendency to play). Analyses probing adversity subtypes and component learning processes revealed dissociable effects:  emotional abuse attenuated normative developmental increases in punishment learning rate, and physical abuse attenuated normative increases in both go bias and a summary score of reward learning (FDR < .05). In contrast, analyses relying on a gross summary score of net success and composite variables representing broad adversity dimensions (abuse vs. neglect) obscured these effects, highlighting the need for fine-grained characterization of both adversity and learning processes. Additionally, adversity did not predict differences in net success, suggesting that youth high and low in adversity reach similar behavioral outcomes through divergent learning strategies. This study is among the first to identify unique effects of different adversity types on specific component processes of valenced learning during adolescence.

title: Minimum Cost Psychological Defense: Theoretical Construction and Anti-Fraud Application
description: Human decision-making under acute stress reveals a critical paradox in applied social psychology: despite possessing adequate declarative risk knowledge, individuals frequently exhibit a knowing-doing gap when confronting high-pressure digital deception. Traditional interventions demanding substantial cognitive resources often fail when deceivers hijack victims' limited cognitive bandwidth via emotional manipulation. To address this, we propose the Minimum Cost Psychological Defense (M.C.P.D.) model. In Study 1, using a mixed-methods design (N = 679), we developed and validated the Public Anti-Fraud Skill Scale. It comprises three dimensions—emotional buffering, proactive defense, and distributed help-seeking—which significantly predicted reduced real-world victimization risk. In Study 2, a double-blind randomized controlled trial (N = 185) evaluated an emergency guide that encodes complex verification tasks into simple implementation intentions. Compared to traditional education, this micro-skill intervention significantly reduced compliance impulses under simulated high-pressure fraud scenarios and shortened decision-making response times, indicating successful cognitive offloading. By transforming declarative knowledge into procedural, automated micro-skills, the M.C.P.D. strategy effectively bypasses executive function limitations. This shifts the anti-fraud paradigm from trait assessment to dynamic behavioral blocking, providing significant contributions to dual-process theory and a scalable tool for practical interventions.

title: Intercorrelations of Paraphilic Interests in a General Population and a Pedophilic Subsample
description: Previous studies have found positive associations between a wide range of paraphilic interests, but recent research on a sample of users of child sexual abuse material suggested that pedophilic interest may be unrelated to other paraphilic interests. In the present study, we investigate the intercorrelation of paraphilic interests in a representative sample of the general population of the United States. Network analysis indicated that paraphilic interests, including pedophilic interests, were positively associated with each other and could be meaningfully grouped based on consent.  However, when examining a subsample of people exhibiting at least some pedophilic interest, the association between pedophilic interest and other paraphilias was substantially weaker. Further research with specially recruited populations will be necessary to explain this phenomenon, but we speculate that this observed difference between the full sample and pedophilic subsample may be explained by a related third variable (e.g., hypersexuality).

title: EEG dynamics during prolonged Samatha meditation:  A case study across levels of meditation expertise
description: This case study examined differences in the temporal dynamics of EEG indices as a function of meditation expertise using a quantitative analytic framework. EEG was recorded and analyzed across multiple sessions over three days during prolonged Samatha meditation in two meditation novices and two expert meditators. Alpha-band power, beta-band power, and peak alpha frequency were extracted in 1-min bins and analyzed using linear mixed-effects models. These models revealed significant expertise × time interactions for beta-band power and peak alpha frequency. Within-group analyses showed that beta-band power decreased over time in the novice group, whereas beta-band power and peak alpha frequency increased over time in the expert group. These findings suggest that EEG indices during prolonged Samatha meditation may exhibit temporal dynamics that differ according to meditation expertise. The results also highlight the utility of analyzing EEG during meditation as a temporally unfolding process rather than only as session-level averages. In addition, a supplementary analysis using an external dataset was conducted to compare the present findings and to examine the applicability of the analytic approach. The supplementary analysis revealed patterns that differed from those observed in the primary dataset. This discrepancy underscores the need to consider factors such as meditation style, practice duration, participant characteristics, and measurement context when interpreting inconsistent findings in meditation EEG research.

title: Sustained Attention in the Presence of a Smartphone:  Does Reward Expectation Matter?
description: Empirical evidence on whether the presence of a smartphone reduces attentional resources is mixed, suggesting additional factors are involved. The current study examines one such factor, namely the presence (or absence) of reward expectations. In a lab experiment, 111 participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions (no smartphone present, smartphone present, or smartphone present with induced reward-message expectation). They completed a Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART) before and after the manipulation. Results indicate that the presence of a smartphone reduced reaction-time performance on the SART, but that reward expectations did not lead to an additional performance decrement. Exploratory analysis indicated that individuals higher in online vigilance showed greater attentional disruption in both the smartphone presence and reward expectation conditions. Together, these findings suggest that smartphone-related attentional costs may depend more on reward history and stable individual differences than on momentary reward anticipation.

title: What is Evaluative Conditioning Anyway? A Response to De Houwer (2026)
description: We respond to De Houwer's (2026) commentary on our preprint (Corneille et al., 2026). In that preprint, we proposed a new definition of the Evaluative Conditioning effect (ECe) to address what we see as limitations in how ECe is currently conceptualized and studied. In the present response, we discuss substantive agreements and disagreements with De Houwer's commentary and clarify apparent misunderstandings on both sides. In doing so, we argue that our proposed definition withstands De Houwer's counter-arguments and offers a sensible alternative to existing definitions. More broadly, we believe that this exchange highlights important conceptual, methodological, and measurement-related questions whose resolution may have far-reaching implications for EC research. We hope that the discussion of these issues lives on beyond this exchange and helps move the field forward.

title: Interictal Epileptiform Discharges and Long-Term Neurodevelopmental Outcomes in Children with Early-Onset Epilepsy: A Systematic Review and Narrative Synthesis
description:
Background: Children with early-onset epilepsy (seizure onset ≤ 5 years) are at elevated risk of adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes, including cognitive impairment, intellectual disability, and autism spectrum features. Seizure burden alone provides limited prognostic information. Interictal epileptiform discharges (IEDs), spike or sharp-wave complexes occurring between clinical seizures on electroencephalography (EEG), are common in paediatric epilepsy and may reflect brain network instability relevant to neurodevelopment. Whether interictal EEG features recorded in early childhood are associated with long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes remains unclear.
Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with a prospectively registered protocol (PROSPERO: CRD420251162420) and reported per PRISMA 2020 guidelines. MEDLINE, Embase, PubMed, PsycINFO, and Scopus were searched from 1966 to October 2025. Longitudinal studies examining interictal EEG features in children with epilepsy onset ≤ 5 years, with neurodevelopmental outcomes assessed ≥12 months after baseline EEG, were eligible. Risk of bias was assessed using QUIPS; certainty of evidence using GRADE.
Results: Of 1,613 records identified, four longitudinal studies met inclusion criteria (epilepsy contexts: infant-onset focal epilepsy, Sturge–Weber syndrome, epileptic encephalopathies, cryptogenic generalised epilepsies). Across all four studies, greater severity or disruption of interictal EEG activity showed a directional association with poorer neurodevelopmental outcomes. All four studies were rated at moderate to high risk of bias. Certainty of evidence was low to very low across all outcome domains.
Conclusions: Directional evidence links interictal EEG severity to neurodevelopmental outcomes in early-onset epilepsy. However, the evidence base is too sparse, insufficiently standardised, and of too low methodological quality to support definitive prognostic inference. Standardised, state-resolved IED metrics and harmonised outcome batteries are needed to clarify the prognostic role of interictal activity in early-onset epilepsy.
Keywords: epilepsy; interictal epileptiform discharges; electroencephalography; prognosis; neurodevelopment; cognition; paediatric

title: Psychosocial Wellbeing Assessment Tools for Children with Cerebral Palsy and Their Families:  A Rapid Review
description: Cerebral palsy is a lifelong motor diagnosis which is primarily associated with impaired movement, balance, and posture. Early intervention programs are widely encouraged to alleviate the physical, psychosocial, social and economic burden of these functional challenges. Despite the benefit of these early interventions, there are no standardised quantitative assessment measures or guidelines as to how to measure the benefits. We conducted and now report a rapid review of the applications of quantitative psychosocial wellbeing assessment tools for children with cerebral palsy under six and their families between 2017 and 2025. This rapid review was done as part of a charity, funded project to measure and assess their early interventions. After searching PsycINFO, Medline, CINAHL databases we identified and extracted data from 113 articles and assessed the utility of 34 free to access psychometric tests identified within them. While this review helped us to identify measures for our charity project, we also identify inconsistencies with reporting theoretical models and the factor validity of current measures, critique the lack of transparency in the data collection procedures reported and commiserate the number of inaccessible, paywalled measures that impair the measurement and assessment of wellbeing of children with cerebral palsy and their families.

title: Parent experiences of holistic early intervention for children with motor learning impairments: A reflexive thematic analysis of Dame Vera Lynn Children’s Charity case studies
description: Early intervention services are widely recommended to support children with motor learning impairments. These services are recommended to be family-centred, and support the parent alongside the child, rather than child at the expense of the parent. In absence of any qualitative reports of holistic early intervention programs within the UK healthcare system, we present our thematic analysis of case studies from Dame Vera Lynn Children’s Charity (DVLCC). We generated four themes of parent experiences: 1.) coming to the DVLCC in distress; 2.) holistic approach to support at DVLCC; 3.) improved child wellbeing; and 4.) improved parent wellbeing. Together, these themes present DVLCC support as a transformational experience for children and their families, supporting them with holistic, relational, family-orientated early intervention services where public health systems lack the resources. Future research is needed to qualitatively and quantitative support these findings, and to economically rationalise early intervention services in the UK.

title: The Joint Effects of Measurement Error and Model Misspecification on Statistical Interactions
description: Measurement error in predictors and unmodelled interaction effects pose recurring challenges in psychological research, yet their joint consequences for regression analysis have not been analytically characterized. We show that classical measurement error and the omission of a true interaction effect are not independent sources of bias, but become entangled such that their combined effects cannot be recovered by applying standard corrections for either problem alone. Specifically, we derive closed-form expressions for the probability limits of OLS regression weights under joint measurement error and interaction omission, characterize the implied asymptotic residual structure, and establish expressions for model fit and prediction error for infinite samples. A key finding is that the residual contributions from measurement error and interaction omission can partially cancel each other out, so that prediction error reduces marginally while leaving coefficient bias large. As a consequence, qualitatively different models can become difficult to distinguish by standard fit metrics, even in large samples. Furthermore, we demonstrate via Monte Carlo simulation that models with more biased estimates can exhibit lower estimator variance, meaning misspecified models may dominate in both inferential and model selection procedures. These results have direct implications for the replicability and interpretability of linear regression models in psychological research.

title: Traditional progressive tests with stopping rules from Computerized Adaptive Testing: Do potential advantages outweigh added complexity?
description: Progressive tests, where items are administered in order of increasing difficulty and test administration is stopped after the respondent has made a prespecified number of errors, are commonplace in psychological and educational practice. The Wechsler intelligence test batteries consist, for instance, of subtests that are based upon this classical adaptive testing format. Stopping a progressive test once frequent errors start to occur makes intuitive sense as the test would, by construction, only get more difficult. Yet, it is unclear how a stopping rule in terms of the number of errors translates into the desired measurement precision and accuracy, and it remains questionable whether stopping everyone after the same number of errors is warranted. In contrast, in modern computerized adaptive testing (CAT), tests are stopped on an individual basis as a function of measurement precision and/or accuracy. In this study, we assess the suitability of three popular CAT-based stopping rules (Change-in-Point-Estimate, Fixed-Precision, and Precision-Gain rules) for use in progressive tests and compare their performance to traditional consecutive-errors stopping rules. The extent to which CAT-based stopping rules can be translated into the simple but transparent count-of-errors metric of traditional stopping rules was explored. Results are discussed from the perspective of the differences in design and test adaptivity between progressive tests and CATs. We conclude with general considerations and implications for optimizing stopping strategies in progressive tests.

title: On Why Meta-d′ May Not Go the Distance: Confidence Distance as a Calibration-Based Measure of Metacognition
description: Meta-d' is widely used to quantify metacognitive sensitivity, and the meta-d'/d' ratio, commonly referred to as the M-ratio, is interpreted as metacognitive efficiency. However, both depend on assumptions inherited from signal detection theory, including Gaussian evidence distributions and a shared latent evidence axis for type-1 decisions and confidence judgments. These assumptions are not guaranteed in many behavioral paradigms. The present paper examines how violations of these assumptions affect meta-d' and the M-ratio, and introduces Confidence Distance as a calibration-based measure of metacognitive performance. Confidence Distance is defined as the mean absolute distance between trial-wise confidence and objective correctness after mapping confidence ratings onto the unit interval. Across simulation studies, non-Gaussian evidence distributions distorted the M-ratio even when metacognitive monitoring was ideal. These distortions were not limited to underestimation: heavy-tailed and light-tailed evidence inflated the ratio, whereas skewed evidence reduced it. In addition, when confidence was based on evidence not used for the type-1 response, meta-d' itself became inflated despite unchanged metacognitive ability. Confidence Distance, by contrast, remained comparatively stable across distributional forms, increased monotonically when metacognitive noise was added, and remained well-defined across multidimensional and multi-alternative task structures because it requires only trial-wise correctness and confidence. These findings suggest that meta-d' and the M-ratio conflate metacognitive performance with distributional misspecification or evidence-axis mismatch in applied research contexts. Confidence Distance therefore offers a transparent and assumption-light index of confidence–accuracy correspondence.

title: Flexible Multiple Imputation of Missing Data in Time-Structured Longitudinal Designs
description: Missing data are common in longitudinal designs and are often addressed with multiple imputation (MI), either as single-level MI, which treats repeated measures as separate variables, or multilevel MI, which treats repeated measures as nested within participants. Previous research has shown that both approaches can perform well in time-structured designs but has largely focused on growth modeling applications, where the assumptions underlying multilevel MI were met. In the present article, we argue that single-level MI is a more flexible method for handling missing data in time-structured designs that requires fewer assumptions and can accommodate a wider range of analyses than multilevel MI. In this context, we also consider applications of single-level MI to longitudinal multiple-indicator designs, in which single-level MI can be extended with composite scores or dimension reduction techniques such as partial least squares (PLS) to accommodate the potentially large number of variables in these types of designs. Our results from two simulation studies suggest that single-level MI provides a flexible treatment of missing data and that PLS in particular can facilitate single-level MI in applications with many variables. We conclude by discussing implications for applied research and by illustrating the application of single-level MI in an empirical example.

title: Culture in psychology and neuroscience: Concepts, relevance, and empirical evidence in rhythm perception
description: Perceptual systems adapt through individual experience across the lifespan, an ability referred to as plasticity. To understand perceptual plasticity, a promising avenue is to investigate how perception is shaped by cultural experience, as a process deeply embedded within collective practices of cultural production and social learning. The current review synthesizes findings from recent behavioral experiments investigating cross-cultural variation in rhythm perception. Specifically, these studies show that fundamental perceptual processes, such as event timing and rhythm categorization, display shared features but also systematic differences across cultural groups. Critically, these differences correlate with statistically prominent and socially relevant features of cultural production, revealing how perceptual systems are tuned to their music-cultural environments. Yet, how can cross-cultural differences in perception be related back to the collective practices that produce the diversity of cultural environments in the first place? To bridge this gap, we propose perceptual niche construction as an evolutionary approach that positions culture as both a source and a product of perceptual plasticity. That is, cultural experience tunes individual perception, yielding culturally diverse perceptual processes. These processes, in turn, create selection pressures shaping cultural production across nested timescales, resulting in diverse cultural environments. This approach presents implications for research in psychology and neuroscience, notably in proposing to operationalize culture as communities of learning and practice. Moreover, it highlights the relevance of contextually situated research, in view of accounting for the dynamic nature of culture-driven perceptual plasticity.

title: Do Emotional Faces Modulate Pupillary Pseudoneglect?
description: Pseudoneglect, a subtle attentional bias toward the left visual field in neurologically healthy individuals, can be measured with a split-screen method in which a bright stimulus in one hemifield elicits stronger pupil constriction when it appears on the left than on the right (Strauch et al., 2022). Replications of this method have reported mixed results, including a large Registered Report that replicated the effect with a small effect size (d =  0.31). Given strong left-field biases in the emotional chimeric face test, the present study (n = 53) combined this task with split-screen pupillometry to test whether emotional face processing amplifies pupillary pseudoneglect. Pupillary pseudoneglect was observed during control trials with non-faces (d =  0.36) and was stronger for emotional faces (d =  0.76), but this difference was not significant. A strong left-field bias was confirmed for emotional face judgements (d =  0.76), which did not correlate with pupillary bias (ρ =  0.26), and for a line bisection task (d =  0.47), which also did not correlate with pupillary pseudoneglect (ρ =  0.19). These data support the split-screen pupillometric method for measuring pseudoneglect and replicate a previously-noted pattern that leftward biases across tasks show little statistical relation to one another.

title: The Body Problem: Cautionary Notes on the Embodied Self
description: Recent years have seen a decisive turn in neuroscience toward embodiment, shifting focus from a brain-bound view of the mind to a more holistic one where the body is treated as constitutive of self and consciousness. While this turn has yielded valuable insights through studies on interoception, multisensory integration and bodily ownership, it also carries significant theoretical and ethical risks. This essay identifies the body problem: the risk of collapsing selfhood into bodyhood, with troubling consequences when applied to bodies that are marginalized or that otherwise do not meet the status quo. Tracing historical debates on mind–body relations, clarifying contemporary notions of the self and examining empirical findings on embodiment, it is argued that strong formulations of the embodied self risk reviving old reductions whereby “less body” implies “less self.” Such reductions threaten to undermine the value of persons whose bodies diverge from normative assumptions. Rather than rejecting embodiment research, this essay calls for caution against its uncritical extension, underscoring the need to guard against theoretical slippage that may entail harmful social consequences.

title: Whole-brain anatomical and temporal patterns  hypothesized to be evoked during a false-belief fMRI task
description:
Background: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) investigations of theory of 
mind and false-belief processing have been central to cognitive neuroscience for several 
decades. Drawing on previous fMRI studies, we (1) hypothesize that a task-general brain 
mode (DMB) will emerge during the false-belief task, (2) specify its anatomical properties, 
and (3) hypothesize how it will respond, in terms of blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) 
signal changes, to task conditions in a false-belief task.
Task: The false-belief task involved story processing involving either theory of mind or only 
physical properties, resulting in two conditions: (1) Mental, and (2) Physical.
Anatomical: The spatial patterns for Default Mode B (DMB; Lemon, Headlights, Flying 
Seagull, Tripod and Angel Wings) are provided in Figure 1.
Temporal: DMB is predicted to activate during the Mental condition due to engagement of 
theory of mind processes, and to deactivate during the Physical condition. Both are predicted 
to involve a mid-trial peak activation or deactivation. Details are presented in the text below 
and Figure 3.
Analysis: Constrained Principal Component Analysis for fMRI (fMRI-CPCA), an 
application of multivariate multiple regression with a finite impulse response model of the 
task timing, followed by dimensionality reduction, will be used to test these hypotheses.
Conclusions: For the false-belief task, DMB is hypothesized to emerge and show specific 
spatial and temporal characteristics. Although examined within the context of false-belief 
tasks, this mode is not specific to this task. Rather, it is part of a broader set of task-general 
functional brain modes observed across multiple cognitive tasks. This approach may provide 
insight into cognitive processes detectable by fMRI underlying theory of mind.
